summary: McKenzie discusses how entering overcrowded software markets (backup apps, photo organizers, project management) is a losing strategy unless you find a differentiating angle. He advocates for niching down to serve a specific, addressable audience rather than competing as a generic commodity. He highlights ProjectThingy as an example of smart differentiation: project management software that can be embedded in your own website, appealing to technically disinclined small businesses. The creator's portfolio of embeddable apps demonstrates how a single differentiating concept can be applied across multiple products.
key_points: Overcrowded markets like backup apps, photo organizers, and project management tools should generally be avoided unless you have a differentiating angle
Niching down (e.g., 'World's Best Photo Organizer For Wedding Photographers') creates an addressable market of customers eager to pay
ProjectThingy differentiates in the crowded project management space by being embeddable in customers' own websites, appealing to non-technical SMEs
A portfolio approach of multiple embeddable apps sharing the same differentiating concept can compound the advantage
money_quotes: The trouble with getting into an uber-crowded market, in a niche which is essentially a commodity service, is making yourself heard above the din.
So instead of being Generic Photo Organizer #1278, maybe you could be World's Best Photo Organizer For Wedding Photographers.
This allows you to carve out a little niche which is addressable (i.e. you have some way of reaching them) and which will leap at the chance to pay you money.

It is *yawn* project management software. But it is project management software which you can embed in your own website, and I'm convinced that makes all the difference in the world to a certain population of technically disinclined SMEs.
Rails is after all 'Basecamp, minus the domain expertise'.
key_insight: In crowded software markets, the winning strategy is not to build a better generic product but to find a specific differentiating angle that makes your product uniquely valuable to an addressable niche.
